one_line_takeaway: A logic-guided fuzzing + LTL-oracle framework builds an executable benchmark showing that state-of-the-art LLM agents will complete tool-use tasks while silently skipping mandatory regulatory safety steps, especially when they must plan from a high-level goal rather than an explicit workflow.
key_finding: Across 13 evaluated LLMs (8 reported in the main figures), larger/more capable models achieve better functional correctness but frequently prioritize task completion over safety, producing non-compliant 'Unsafe Success'. Compliance collapses when the workflow scaffolding is removed: GPT-5 drops from 75% Pass@1 (Workflow) to 28% (Goal) in Smart Home IoT. Performance is strongly modulated by 'API Density' (number of safety-critical tools) — GPT-5 is near-perfect in Financial Services (4 safety APIs) but degrades badly in Smart Home IoT (10 safety APIs). Scaling does not reliably fix compliance: the GPT-5 series shows a positive scaling trend (bigger = fewer violations) while the Gemini family defies it (Gemini-Pro often worse than Gemini-Flash, e.g., 57% adherence violations in Tele-Healthcare). Semantic (API-grounding) errors persist even in frontier models (Gemini-Pro 50% semantic failure in Financial Services; Gemini-Flash 80% in Smart Home IoT) while syntax errors are negligible. Separately, the logic-guided fuzzer beats an LLM-only generation baseline on test diversity, hitting 100% Safety-Critical API Coverage in all three domains.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LOGISAFETYGEN, an automated framework that converts unstructured regulatory documents plus tool specifications into deterministic Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces (LTLf) oracles, then uses a logic-guided (constraint-satisfaction) fuzzer to synthesize executable, compliant ground-truth tool-invocation traces, and finally a 'safety masking' step that rewrites those traces into natural-language instructions with the mandatory safety steps deliberately omitted. Instantiating this framework yields LOGISAFETYBENCH, a 240-task, human-verified benchmark (120 paired goal/workflow samples) across three high-stakes domains. The central object being measured is 'implicit regulatory compliance': whether an LLM agent, given only a business goal and the raw policy text (with explicit safety instructions stripped out), will autonomously insert the mandatory compliance/safety operations. The authors also propose a Dual-Oracle evaluation (functional oracle + LTL safety oracle) that lets them isolate 'Unsafe Success' — cases where the model completes the business task while violating a regulatory constraint. NOTE: benchmarks like ToolEmu, Agent-SafetyBench, AgentBench, ShieldAgent, StateEval, RuleArena, EU-Agent-Bench are cited as related work / baselines, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: This is a safety-evaluation paper, not an adversarial-attack paper. The 'threat' is passive non-compliance under benign conditions: an autonomous LLM tool-using agent, acting on an ordinary user request, omits or misorders mandatory regulatory safety steps (e.g., granting account access without first performing an authentication/verification check, or acting on health records without required consent/authorization gates). The authors explicitly distinguish this from adversarial/malicious-request benchmarks (AgentHarm, ToolSword) and from runtime defenses like ShieldAgent — LOGISAFETYGEN is an offline pre-deployment stress test. The regulatory constraints are anchored in real policies: EU PSD2 (financial), US HIPAA Security Rule (tele-healthcare), and ETSI EN 303 645 (consumer IoT). The most dangerous observed outcome is 'Unsafe Success': functionally correct task completion that nonetheless breaks a compliance rule.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) Representation limits of the safety oracles — restricting to two LTL templates captures only the temporal ORDERING of API calls, so the framework cannot express parameter-value or data-flow constraints (e.g., verifying a transfer amount is within a safe limit, or detecting malicious payloads in otherwise-valid calls); policies needing deep semantic inspection or probabilistic judgment cannot be formalized. (2) Dependence on generator capability — initial scenario diversity is bounded by the creative priors of the backbone LLM (GPT-5-Mini), and purely automated generation struggles with extreme structural novelty without human guidance. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: the benchmark is only 240 tasks over three domains; oracle construction and instruction synthesis both use GPT-5-Mini, so the pipeline that grades models shares a model family with one of the strongest models under test; and the tool environments are synthetic mock sandboxes adapted from ToolEmu rather than live production systems.

datasets_used: LOGISAFETYBENCH (this paper's own benchmark): 240 human-verified tasks / 120 paired goal-vs-workflow samples across three domains
Financial Services domain grounded in EU Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2), 4 safety-critical APIs
Tele-Healthcare domain grounded in US HIPAA Security Rule, 7 safety-critical APIs
Smart Home IoT domain grounded in ETSI EN 303 645, 10 safety-critical APIs
Tool specifications adapted from ToolEmu; GPT-5-Mini used as backbone for policy extraction and instruction synthesis
quantitative_results: Benchmark size: 240 tasks (120 paired samples), 40 valid paired samples targeted per scenario across 3 scenarios
Human verification acceptance: 73.9% aggregate for safety-oracle (LTL) construction; 70.6% for instruction/masking test tuples
Safety-Critical API Coverage (Ours): 100% in all three domains vs GPT-5-mini baseline 85.7% (Tele-Healthcare), 100% (Financial), 80% (Smart Home IoT)

Adjacent Transition Coverage, Ours vs GPT-5-mini (Table 2): Tele-Healthcare 42.6% vs 28.4%; Financial Services 85.6% vs 23.4%; Smart Home IoT 63.3% vs 30.1% (note: prose mislabels the largest ATC gain as Tele-Healthcare '23.4% vs 85.6%', but Table 2 attributes those figures to Financial Services)
GPT-5 Pass@1 in Smart Home IoT: 75% (Workflow) vs 28% (Goal)
Gemini-Pro: 57% Instruction-Adherence violations in Tele-Healthcare (Goal); 50% Semantic-Error rate in Financial Services
Gemini-Flash: 80% Semantic-Error rate in Smart Home IoT
13 LLMs evaluated total; 8 representative models reported in main text
baselines_compared: GPT-5-mini prompted to generate diverse API programs under the same constraints (LLM-only generation baseline, for coverage/diversity comparison)
Prior benchmarks contrasted qualitatively in Table 1: ToolSword, TAI3, ToolEmu, Agent-SafetyBench (on axes of automated valid trace, implicit safety, formal oracle, executable environment)
Models under test (Pass@1): GPT-5, GPT-5-Mini, GPT-5-Nano, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-Flash, Gemini-Lite, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B, Llama-3.1-8B (plus Qwen-Coder and others among the 13)
